insights: Incident volume increased by 14% from the first half (35.67/month) to the second half (40.67/month) of the 13-month period, confirming a statistically meaningful upward trend in demand.

The increasing incident trend is uniformly distributed across all agents, categories, and assignment groups—each unique value in these dimensions received exactly 100 incidents, indicating zero workload concentration or differential pressure.

No compositional shift occurred between halves: category, agent, and group distributions remained stable within ±3 percentage points, confirming the growth reflects broad-based demand expansion rather than reassignment or classification drift.

Because workload is perfectly balanced across all operational units, the rising incident volume translates directly into proportional, system-wide pressure on capacity—no single agent, category, or group is absorbing disproportionate impact.

The absence of imbalance means agent efficiency implications cannot be attributed to uneven assignment; any observed efficiency changes must stem from factors external to assignment distribution, such as process changes, tooling, or macro-level demand complexity.
summary: The analysis confirms a clear 14% increase in monthly incident volume from the first to second half of the 13-month period, signaling sustained growth in service demand. Critically, this growth is perfectly uniform across all agents, categories, and assignment groups—each received exactly 100 incidents—eliminating workload concentration as a driver of efficiency variation. The stability in compositional distribution (±3 pp shift) further confirms that the trend reflects broad-based demand expansion, not structural reallocation. As a result, rising volume imposes proportional, system-wide capacity pressure rather than exposing bottlenecks in specific teams or domains. Next, focus should shift to evaluating whether agent-level handling time, closure rate, or backlog accumulation has deteriorated in tandem with the overall volume increase—since assignment fairness is confirmed, efficiency analysis must now target process, tooling, or complexity drivers.
